comment: People tend to assume salaries are about comparable dollar for dollar.  This is understandable at the lower-end where differences are not significant enough to be noticeable.  As HR responsible for SG/MY, I have analysed the confidential pay scales of both countries across a variety of industries and the general difference in salaries for jobs of similar size is 1.6 : 1 (MY:SG), on average.  Meaning, if you have a job that pays SGD1k p.m. in Singapore, the same "sized" job will be pay RM1.6k in Malaysia.  So, if consumer goods cost 2-3x more in MY but you are paid only 1.6x more, that is why people feel money tends to go further in SG.  The bigger numbers in MY also tend to push you to the higher tier of taxes, thus further reducing your nett income.

comment: The system isn’t about privilege for the native, that’s what the uneducated thinks because it’s simpler to understand. It isn’t about who invited whom. We are all equal, we are all Malaysians. The incentives are about economics. As part of the British divide and conquer tactics, the races are segregated to prevent unity against the ruler minority. Natives were kept in the village, the Chinese in the mining/trade, the educated Indians in bureaucracy and the uneducated Indians in the plantations. Since different sector benefited by different amount economically, there was economic inequality between the races and the animosity in the country lead to racial riot in 1969. Economic policies were introduced by the government voted by the majority race who created incentives to try level the economics disparity. It’s a lot closer now although it’s quite obvious if you look around you in Malaysia which ethnicity is still head and shoulders above everyone, despite the economic preference for another ethnicity. People like to believe that this is due to their inherent characteristic to overcome challenges but the truth is, they benefited from generational and systematic wealth advantage for the original British policy. When your family can afford to send you to the best schools, you get given great jobs by your family/your racially identical neighbours or the systemic racism of “mandarin speakers only” recruitment policy, it’s not hard to continue the economic success of your ethnic group vs the other group who had no opportunity for education, no networking and no money. The government offered incentives that promoted inclusivity such as minimum quota into education to make sure the majority race isn’t left behind in education. Same with businesses. The government created networking opportunity by sheltering a tiny portion of the economy so that new start ups can eventually participate in the broader economy. Evidently it’s successful. Beside the grumblings of the uneducated and the selfish, the races live in harmony and the country thrives economically compared to its neighbours bar SG. Countries that did not practice affirmative actions and where the minority hold the majority of the wealth, ended up with violence or oppression for the minority such as apartheid in SA, the Chinese in Indonesia and Indians in Kenya. The only sad part is that the uneducated, minority Indians are being left behind economically in the economic battle between the rich, established minority vs the poorer, up and coming majority race.

comment:
I was born & bred in Malaysia, left at age 15 (1980) because the unequal rights of Malays/Muslims & non-Malays (non-Muslims). It got so bad that 80% (if not more) of my generation in similar socio-economics left, for the same reasons. We knew there was next to no chance of us getting into local universities, gov't jobs, sort after business permits, lucrative gov't contracts, etc. There are about 40 of us in my generation (first cousins only), all those beyond high-school education (>30) were educated overseas & didn't return for good. All, except one, graduated with at least one degree & landed great jobs globally. We all established roots & took citizenship elsewhere. BTW, Malaysia does not allow dual citizenship. My father/uncles used to say kids in my generation, who chose to remain in Malaysia was either of super wealthy family (able to buy their way through life) OR have no way out (can't afford/not smart enough for scholarship). We were told to leave the comfort of home as soon as were deemed mature enough. The well intended Affirmative Action clause for Bumiputras/Malays in the Constitution when negotiating independence from Britain was miss-used (surprise! surprise!), resulting in a country with "two tier of citizenship rights". Things have improved over the years because the economy has improved since the discovery of oil. But it's still pretty bad, or the Brain Drain won't be so drastic & on-going.... The gov't is doing all it could, trying to entice Malaysians like me to return (loyalty to your birth country, fast track career, financial rewards, etc). So far, no one I know took the offer. A few wanting to live in Malaysia (with aging parents) just use the avenues mentioned in the video & kept their foreign passport!!! Visiting "back home" gets increasingly less as the older generation passes on. 

If you are a white foreigner from a wealthy country living among the "well to do's" in Malaysia, you won't be exposed to all this ugliness because it's a privilege lifestyle, any social issues or unjust will be nicely tucked away.......  

Do visit Malaysia because it is a beautiful country & wonderful as a foreigner. I'll swing by for its amazing food when I escape the cold for a few weeks this Winter. @tyjay6885 You seem to have missed the point. In the point you made, you've compared apples to oranges. And even in the mis-comparison, you still made no-point. So let me clarify. 

Firstly, the point I made is that the policy was made for economics equality, not overall economic performance. Through economic equallity, racial harmony was achieved. No repeat of the racial riot of 1969. Incidentally, economic equality actually also bring about net economic prosperity to the country as it taps into all of it's human resource, instead of granting opportunity only to the rich one ethnic class. Secondly, comparing Malaysia and SG is apples to oranges. Malaysia was a wealth in the hands of the minority, SG is wealth in the hands of the majority. It's not equivalent, so need different solutions. Thirdly SG is successful with no affirmative/privilege action, well, Yemen doesnt have affirmative/privilege action either and it's amongst the poorest. So your point is not really a point at all.

Unfortunately you're wrong about benefiting from the privilege system as I left the country aged 11. Went to the same gov. school and clinics as everyone. I returned a few years ago and ran a business. There is no government presence in my industry so again, no benefits. However, I did face racial hostility as my market is exclusively from non-bumi, supplier and customers. I paid more for supply and had to sell for less, else they'll go to someone of the same ethnic class if the price is the same. But it's ok, with my oversea experience, we still do ok. The suppliers and customers now treat me almost equally, the bond of trust and respect trumps over hatred & tribalism. 

Anecdotal evidence is not reality or even relevant unfortunately. You can make up anecdotal evidence to say anything you want. What I wrote are verifiable facts and logic so you're welcome to read up and educate yourself. Starts with the 1970 NEP - which was supposed eliminate racism through equallity in equity for all which is demonstrability proven in today's Malaysia compared to 1969 Malaysia.

In fact, NEP policies is generally inclusive, rather than exclusive. It forces housing areas to have a mix, rather than turn into a wealthy, single race ghetto/gated community. It forced universities to do the same. Before NEP, most races do not live together, do not go to same schools and do not work in the same places (although this is still the case by those company's hiding behind the "mandarin speaker only" policy).

Unfortunately, abolishing it is not equality, it just benefits the rich via the generational and systematic wealth advantage. comment:
I think KL is my overall favorite Asian city. I appreciate the hint of British influence leftover there, yet you never forget you're in Asia. It is gritty enough to be interesting at times, but not something you cannot escape to live a more comfortable Western life. The food is probably my favorite overall in Asia. Obviously the value for money proposition is hard to beat. 

The downside for me is the time zone. I do business in North America and no matter how you try and approach it, matching up KL's night to North America's business day is going to leave you sleep-deprived. Also... "graysia". comment: It's worth mentioning that the highway (motorway) networks thruout the Peninsular are good and well maintained. Easily one of the best in the world. You can stay for three months straight in Malaysia and if you want to extend the visa again for 3 months you can do a boarder crossing to Singapore or Thailand. comment:
Thank you for your video about my country.
I am a Malaysia, Chinese have lived here for decades and have seen Malaysia rise, fall, and rise again. Every challenge we have faced has been part of our journey as a growing nation. We don’t develop overnight like Dubai.…..we grow gradually, at our own pace, with our rich and diverse culture.

I’m truly grateful to our Ministry of Education for shaping an education system since the 60s that exposes our children to world history from an early age. Our kids may only know bits and pieces, but they understand there is a wide world out there with different countries, climates, languages, and geographies.

Let me share this…..back in the 80s, some Americans joked that Malaysians still lived in trees …… we just laughed! We have always known about the world outside of Malaysia... it’s just that the world didn’t know much about us. Funny, isn’t it?

Here in Malaysia, we live humbly and peacefully. All we want is stability and decent jobs. Want to be rich, just work harder, maybe work 2 jobs and do a small business like selling Malaysia burgers (many of our youngsters doing that…) Thanks to our government and NGOs, there are CSR support everywhere  no one truly goes hungry here, except maybe a few who choose not to work (which happens everywhere). Even with a minimum wage of around RM1,700, you can still live modestly without going hungry because of the subsidies on essentials like rice, sugar, cooking oil, petrol……you name it….we are lucky.

Of course, some Malaysians move abroad, just personal preference, some enjoy it, many come back home eventually. That’s just part of life. No government is perfect (politics will always be noisy!), but the best thing we can do is stay united and focus on living harmoniously among the PEOPLE down here….

And here’s one thing about Malaysians …..we may argue among ourselves like siblings, but if an outsider criticizes us, we all unite in a heartbeat. We have gone through good and bad times together, and that is the bonding that we build. We only want a peaceful home to live in.

Looking back, I still believe Malaysia is one of the best countries to live in. Sure, I’ve had my share of personal struggles, but nothing changes how I feel about Malaysians are some of the kindest, most humble people on Earth.

We are patience…..you notice we don’t honk unnecessarily when we are driving, we can wait in long Qs for food without complaint, and we even enjoy traffic jams with snacks and coffee! Everyone here is treated equally; no big hierarchies, no arrogance, any status can just jam at ‘mamak’ for ‘the tarik’ we are all the same because we share the same roots, same food, same education in a diverse cultures….the Malay, Chinese, Indian, Sabahan, Sarawakian,…there are more..

There’s so much more I could say, but to sum it up:
I LOVE YOU, MALAYSIA and I love my fellow Malaysians. ❤

comment:
I've been to KL three times and would go back every year if I could. It's excellent value for money and the climate once you get used to the humidity is perfect. Lovely people who speak English and great food.
However there are some cons which strangely expats who make YouTube videos never mention.
Drivers generally do not give way to pedestrians. Many drive straight through red lights. I nearly got run over on a pedestrian crossing by a guy texting on his phone! Crossing roads there is risky business and could lead to your dream vacation being cut short if you don't have your wits about you!!!Despite the glossy veneer it remains third world. You often see and smell piles of rubbish and litter in the poorer areas even within a couple of blocks of Bukit Bintang. At times you are greeted with the smell of raw sewerage despite the fact you can if you hold your nose  think you were in Singapore. Definitely a city of contrasts.
And as a caution I would advise against eating street food considering that I caught food poisoning eating at a five star hotel. Despite the cons it's one of my favourite destinations in Asia but do   be careful crossing roads even at the lights.

comment:
I have lived in KL ~25 years since 1986. I mostly agree with Andrew's statements in this video, but feel like I should share some of my experience;
Property prices; $500k is indeed upscale (well, you can buy $5mill penthouse with 3 floors and your own swimming pool in the sky), but it is possible to get a new decent apartment for $100-150k, even a house if you are prepared to do some renovation (laborers don't earn a lot). All depends on which area, and distance to city center.
Food prices; All about "where". In the video clip, Andrew is walking around in the Bukit Bintang area, which is expat and tourist-y. Food prices there are 3-5 times higher than in the local suburbs (Cheras, Ampang, Kepong/Selayang,...). "Downside" with the local suburbs is that no fancy western food exist at all.
Dating; Used to be great. Went downhill in the late 90s, and probably an art form or pure magic by now.
Business; A lot of the business community is orbiting "clubs", meaning Selangor Golf club, Jockey club, Yacht club, Freemasons/Rotary, and not night clubs. If you are a member elsewhere in the world, it may get you in the door and if accepted, connections get a lot easier. Once "inside" the community, a lot of informal "visiting" at offices happens to expand the network and if you have a few really good friends, they tend to want to introduce you to many others on such "visits" -- don't turn those down.

Never lived there but have worked there on assignments and visited it frequently.

I wonder if you would have liked it when I first visited in the late 80s.
It wasn't full of endless malls, and many still lived in kampongs.
Somehow I liked it better then.  It had more individuality and with less crowding and highrise.
Yet I like Singapore more now than during the same period.
I tend to find a characteristic sameness about the modern parts of Malaysia, though some areas are quite distinct such as KLCC..
Though I'd argue the same about many cities, not just KL.

Malaysia's population is 23% Chinese and much of the industry and trade is controlled by people who are ethnically Chinese.
And obviously, much of the food, art an culture is also Chinese or mixed (what here is called Peranakan).
On the other hand, Singapore is 76% Chinese. 

Yet the races are really not that united.
Since Mahatir's "Malay Dilemma", non-Malay (Bumiputra) people have been actively discriminated against.  Some improvements have been made since then, but the country is far less united than is Singapore.
Further, political corruption, cronyism, incompetence and self-interest are ever-present, and the people just accept that whoever is in power will continue with the same ways.
I can't tolerate that.  I also lived in Indonesia for some years ago, and it was at least as bad.

The cost of living is low largely as incomes are low by Singaporean or western standards.
GDP per person is around USD14,000 in 2025, and basic wages are RMB1,700 a month, which is only USD350 or so.
Yes, there are malls and western shops everywhere but the average workers tend to shop elsewhere.
The times I have worked in Malaysia, I have found it frustrating and sometimes annoying.
From a western perspective, workers were not as dedicated and focused as i hoped.  That does not apply to everyone, but it applies to a lot.
Getting things done was not a simple matter, and I found that I had to waste too much time supervising others, many whom were professionals and well educated.

So to estimate how much you could earn in Singapore, let's use another way since you know many Singaporeans in your line. Yourself as Financial advisor, influencer... perhaps if good enough (performance) could match your Singaporean peers that could own a condo & car, and assuming could get close to that salary. Here're my own insight: 

Renting 1 Bed room & 1 Toilet = RM 4,680
1 year (3rd party) car insurance for a 2012 Toyota Corolla = RM 8,892
1 plate of fried rice = RM 46.80
1 dosa (thosai) = RM 50.7
1 month of Electricity & Water Bill =  RM 1,053 for 2 people
1 gallon (3.78 Litre) of RON 87 gasoline (petrol) = RM 14.62 

No Petrol Subsidy From The Govt. 
No Free / Cheap Healthcare From Govt. Hospital
No Cheap Eat-Out
Clinic Visit Costs Me RM 1,300 easily (in the US) vs RM 100 Only (in Malaysia) For The Same Symptoms (Without Insurance) 
Even With Insurance, It Won't Cover Most Of The Medical Bill
Medical Bill Are So Expensive That A Huge Chunk Of Americans Go Bankrupt / Homeless From Being Sick

Double Income Tax = 1 Federal Tax + 1 State Tax
Compulsory Heavy Tipping culture 10% - 20% (minimum) 
Extra Gratuity 15% -> 16% or so for 4 / 5 people in a restaurant
Extra Charge If You Use Debit or Credit Card

Many Malaysians over here said they prefer to go back to Malaysia no matter their race , religion, gender or etc.

comment: Well, i have been living her for almost 3 years, and i would never recommend it to someone. It is blend and boring AF. Speak about culture, as much as you would expect from a sharia law country. Not even mentioning the racial suppression, discrimination, corruptions, regular protests and some other fun specials like flying during ramadan. Dating, not that i have done it much, is as mentioned also great:  a pool of western fboys, gold diggers and uncultured locals mingling around. It's all about money and social status here.  Traffic is sh*t in every way possible, public transportation a joke and the road conditions terrible. Not to mention that if you touch one of these maniac scooters you can got to jail or even receive the death penalty. Not sayin it happens a lot, but you will get squeezed a lot for money.  Quality of living, especially when it comes the quality of building and pretty much everything locally produced is also terrible. Cannot count how many homes had molding, broken appliances and cheap fake materials all over. Don't expect european quality in anything, even in the higher price bracket. comment:
I’m a Taiwanese and have been living here in KL for more than a year. The overall price level is low (transportation/food/housing,etc) but it all comes with a cost. 

1. Low Food quality. The weather has been too harsh for food to be kept safe and fresh. Four out of ten times (4/10) I got rotten yogurt from a top tier supermarket’s fridge. Hygiene is sometimes an issue (e.g.) water shortages happens occasionally. Besides, people near me always coughing/sneezing somewhere, which got me COVID once.

2. Bad transportation. Traffic jams everywhere and by no means you can avoid that, cuz public transportation seems non-existent, cuz it can’t get you where you want. Even if it does, it’s neither reliable nor stable , high MKBF meaning it gets late often.

3. Better be careful if you’re a non-Muslim single woman. Religion seems overly emphasised to me here in KL. If you love eating pork, don’t like children, dress like a modern lady, and well educated (in a westerner way), then your life will be somewhat difficult in an invisible way.

4. People are generally really nice but super inefficient. For example, I applied for a credit card in a local bank and still haven’t got it yet (still in progress), after a 5-month wait. Yes five months. Just unbelievable in the western world. Better not go grocery shopping during weekends since your milk will prolly go rotten while you wait in a long queue.


All in all, I don’t recommend KL to be a perfect place for the rich who pursues life quality. The income level here in KL limits the possibilities - you can’t easily get “premium” service (tranquil/swift/direct/fresh/hygienic/organic), even if you’re rich. For me, the life here in KL is a bit challenging in my perfectly honest opinion.

A friend more familiar with Malaysia commented to me thus:

"While some of the things he says are true. I think he overlooks the huge elephant in the Malaysian room, the Theocratic Creep

I would suggest thatMalaysia has descended into Confessional Politics

We need not be a professional alarmists to observe that Malaysia’s steady march toward Islamic conservatism represents something rather more sinister than mere cultural evolution. What we are witnessing is nothing less than the quiet strangulation of a once-tolerant society by the twin ligatures of faith and ethnicity, a process made all the more insidious by its bureaucratic respectability and glacial pace.

The ascendancy of Parti Islam Se-Malaysia and UMNO and its ideological cousins should trouble anyone with even a passing acquaintance with history’s lessons about mixing theology with statecraft. 

There is also the unrecognised apartheid. There is not one non-Malay policeman, there is not one non-Malay serving officer in the Armed Forces. There is not one Petronas petrol station owned by anybody other than Malay..

These are not mere political parties in the conventional sense, they are vehicles for the imposition of seventh-century Wahabi jurisprudence upon a diverse, modern society. That such movements gain traction by promising to police hemlines and enforce gender apartheid tells us everything we need to know about the intellectual poverty at their core.
What makes this particularly galling is the way in which this creeping theocracy masquerades as cultural authenticity. 

The notion that Malay identity must be wedded to an increasingly Wahabi interpretation of Islam is as historically illiterate as it is culturally destructive. Millions of Saudi Arabian petrodollars have Set Up religious schools turning out Imams who beat the exclusive cultural drum and traditional Malay culture, syncretic, tolerant, infused with Hindu and Buddhist influences, is being steamrollered by a sterile, imported puritanism that would have been unrecognisable to previous generations. This is not cultural preservation; it is cultural obliteration dressed in pious garments.

You only need to go to Putraja the new capital created by Dr M as a cashcow, to see the architecture is 100% Islamic and Chinese Indian and other influences that made Malaysia what it is today have been airbrushed out of existence.

The bureaucratic entrenchment of Islamic supremacy creates what one might call “soft coercion”, the death by a thousand administrative cuts. When non-Muslims find their businesses hamstrung by religious diktat, when houses of worship become subject to Kafkaesque permitting processes, when the very visibility of their existence becomes a matter for state-sanctioned disapproval, we are no longer discussing mere cultural sensitivity. We are discussing the methodical construction of a two-tier society in which citizenship itself becomes conditional upon religious affiliation.

Most troubling of all is the capture of young minds by an ideology that traffics in absolute certainty and cultivates intolerance as a virtue. When education becomes indoctrination, when critical thinking is subordinated to rote memorisation of religious texts, when political Islam is presented as the only authentic response to modernity’s challenges, we are engineering a future in which reason and pluralism will find themselves increasingly besieged.

The tragedy is that Malaysia once offered a different model, imperfect, certainly, but one in which diverse communities coexisted with reasonable amity. That this is being sacrificed on the altar of religious nationalism should concern not only Malaysia’s minorities, but anyone who believes that the Enlightenment’s hard-won victories over theocratic tyranny remain worth defending.

comment:
You can live in KL for way less than 3000 usd a month. Im renting a one bedroom apartment in a modern complex with all the amenities over in Mont Kiara which is a more premium suburb, for under 2100MYR per month, which includes all utilities and internet. 
No need to waste money on a vehicle as grabs (uber) is very cheap, if you only need to commute every other day or so.

So after that, all you need is money for food. I eat a lot, and i can easily spend less than 50MYR per day eating out, i could probably get by with 25MYR if i really wanted to. 

So 1500MYR per month on food eating out for every meal, and 2100MYR on rent and bills per month comes to around 3600MYR for all the essentials, which ends up being about 910USD a month. If you can earn 2000k USD per month, you can easily save half of that and live a comfortable life, and not pay any tax.

I dont know why there arnt more people doing it.

Keep in mind, this is for a single person.

But you could also rent a bigger place for the same price in a less premium area, which is still very clean and very safe. So you could still potentially raise a family quiet cheaply. And if you buy groceries instead of eating out you could save even more.
